title: Pamphlet-stitch notebook
subtitle: A single-signature notebook sewn with three holes and a card cover
excerpt: The pamphlet stitch is the simplest sewn binding: one signature, a card cover, and three holes. The result is a flat-opening notebook that holds 16 to 32 pages and takes under an hour to make.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on the pamphlet stitch. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock. The three-hole pattern and sewing path described here follow the canonical method unchanged.

glossaryTerms.definition: A group of folded sheets sewn together at the fold to form one section of a book's text block.
A single-signature sewing pattern worked through three or five holes along the fold. The simplest sewn bookbinding structure.
A single sheet folded once to produce two leaves and four pages.

content.text: The pamphlet stitch is the oldest sewn binding still in everyday use. Three holes, one continuous thread, a card cover: the resulting
signature
lies flat when open and takes a few minutes to sew. This version makes a pocket-format notebook from four
folios
of cartridge paper inside a card cover, producing 16 pages. Add more folios for a thicker book, up to about eight before the spine starts to arch.
What you need

attrs.heading: Materials for one A6 pamphlet notebook

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm, A5 (portrait)
items.qty: 4 sheets (each folds to produce one A6 folio)

items.name: Cover card, 300 gsm or heavier, A5
Bookbinding thread, waxed linen, 4-cord
items.qty: Twice the height of the book plus 15 cm

attrs.heading: Cutting tools
attrs.body: Always cut against a metal ruler held with the non-cutting hand at least two finger-widths back from the blade's path, on a self-healing cutting mat.

content.text: Preparing the signature
Fold each A5 sheet in half, short-edge to short-edge, so the fold runs along the long axis. Crease firmly with the bone folder, running it along the entire fold from one end to the other.
Nest the four folded sheets inside each other with their spines aligned. This stack is the
signature
Fold the cover card to match and wrap it around the outside of the signature. Crease it with the bone folder at the spine.
Open the whole stack at the centre fold. Mark three sewing stations on the inner spine: one at the centre, and one 12 mm in from each end. These are the three holes of the
pamphlet stitch
Hold the open signature in a folding jig or grip it firmly while you pierce each station straight through all layers with the bookbinding awl. Work over the cutting mat.
Sewing

Cut a length of thread twice the height of the book plus 15 cm. Run it through the beeswax block twice, then once more. Threading the needle: fold the end of the thread back 4 cm over the needle and grip the fold against the needle shaft with finger and thumb, then draw the needle away so the fold locks in the eye.
Close the signature. Working from the outside of the spine, enter the centre hole and push the needle through to the inside. Pull the thread through, leaving a 5 cm tail on the outside.
From the inside, exit through the top hole (head station). Pull the thread snug.
From the outside, re-enter the centre hole. Pull snug.
From the inside, exit through the tail hole. Pull snug.

From the outside, re-enter the centre hole one final time. The needle exits at the inside next to the original tail. The long thread now runs on the spine; the short tail runs alongside it on one side of the centre stitch.
Tie the long thread and the short tail together with a reef knot, making sure the knot sits on one side of the long centre stitch (not across it). Trim both ends to 8 mm.
Finishing
If the cover overhangs the text block, trim with the craft knife against the metal ruler. Press the closed notebook under a heavy book for an hour to flatten any curl from the cover card.
Care
Store flat or upright with neighbours on either side to support the spine. The single-signature construction is robust for everyday use; the thread is unlikely to break before the paper wears out.
Variations

Five holes instead of three gives the binding a more finished appearance and distributes thread tension across more points. A longer stitch path in the five-hole version: enter the centre, go up to hole 2, back to centre, up to hole 1 (head), back to hole 2, centre, hole 4, centre, hole 5 (tail), hole 4, centre. Tie off beside the original centre exit.
